target.title: fix(runtime): snapshot tool definitions
target.body:
## Summary
- Snapshot plugin command registrations through guarded field reads before validation and storage.
- Reject unreadable command metadata without poisoning later registrations, and copy optional command metadata before storage.
- Snapshot realtime voice tools before provider exposure in both shared Talk merging and OpenAI realtime session construction, so one malformed custom tool/schema is skipped while built-in and healthy tools survive.
- Snapshot Mistral, Anthropic provider, native Anthropic transport, OpenAI-compatible completions, native Ollama, Amazon Bedrock, and Google prompt-cache request tools before SDK/provider payload construction, skip unreadable/proxy-backed schemas for normal requests, and fail closed when required provider `toolChoice` semantics cannot be honored after quarantine.
- Harden the shared plain-text tool-call compatibility wrapper so unreadable tool names do not disable healthy provider stream repair.

## Verification
- `CI=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=30000 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_HEARTBEAT_MS=10000 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/plugins/commands.test.ts --reporter=dot`

- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json src/plugin-sdk/provider-stream-shared.ts src/plugin-sdk/provider-stream-tool-names.test.ts`

- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json src/agents/anthropic-transport-stream.ts src/agents/anthropic-transport-stream.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/google-prompt-cache.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/google-prompt-cache.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local`

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: plugin command registration now handles unreadable command metadata as a failed registration and keeps later healthy commands registerable; stored optional metadata is copied before plugin-owned objects can mutate. Custom realtime voice tools are snapshotted before provider exposure in shared Talk merging and in OpenAI native/browser realtime sessions, so unreadable custom tool metadata or nested schema getters do not abort the realtime tool list or client-secret request body. Mistral, Anthropic provider, native Anthropic transport, OpenAI-compatible completions, native Ollama, Amazon Bedrock, and Google prompt-cache request tools are snapshotted before SDK/provider payload construction, so revoked descriptors, proxy schemas, and nested schema getters are skipped for normal requests while healthy tools remain available; explicit required provider `toolChoice` settings fail closed when they point at quarantined or unavailable tools. The shared plain-text tool-call compatibility wrapper now ignores unreadable tool names while preserving healthy repair names.
Real environment tested: local linked OpenClaw gwt worktree on macOS; Crabbox/Testbox remote gates unavailable until local sparse-sync disk or coordinator/Testbox auth clears.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: ran focused plugin command, realtime voice consult, OpenAI realtime provider, Mistral provider, Anthropic provider, OpenAI-compatible completions provider, SDK provider-stream wrapper, Ollama stream snapshot, Amazon Bedrock stream, native Anthropic transport, and Google prompt-cache Vitest files; scoped oxfmt checks; scoped oxlint checks; `git diff --check`; local Codex autoreview; and retried remote `pnpm check:changed` through Crabbox.
Evidence after fix: `src/plugins/commands.test.ts` passed 51 tests; `src/talk/agent-consult-tool.test.ts` passed 9 tests; `extensions/openai/realtime-voice-provider.test.ts` passed 51 tests; `src/llm/providers/mistral.test.ts` passed 3 tests; `src/llm/providers/anthropic.test.ts` passed 8 tests; `src/llm/providers/openai-completions.test.ts` passed 24 tests; `src/plugin-sdk/provider-stream-tool-names.test.ts` and `extensions/ollama/src/stream-tool-snapshot.test.ts` passed 2 tests across 2 shards; `extensions/amazon-bedrock/stream.runtime.test.ts` passed 9 tests; `src/agents/anthropic-transport-stream.test.ts` passed 53 tests; `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/google-prompt-cache.test.ts` passed 7 tests; oxfmt and oxlint exited clean; autoreview reported no accepted/actionable findings after review-driven fixes.
Observed result after fix: unreadable command `nativeNames` returns a stable registration error and a following healthy command registers; unreadable realtime custom tool names or nested schema getters are skipped while built-in/healthy tools remain provider-visible; OpenAI native websocket `session.update` and browser client-secret bodies preserve healthy tool schemas without throwing on bad siblings; Mistral, Anthropic provider, native Anthropic transport, OpenAI-compatible completions, native Ollama, Amazon Bedrock, and Google prompt-cache skip unreadable/proxy-backed request tools while preserving healthy tool payloads; required provider tool-choice settings fail closed instead of downgrading to unconstrained generation when the required tool is quarantined.
What was not tested: broad `pnpm check:changed` has not completed remotely in this environment; `node scripts/crabbox-wrapper.mjs run --label check-changed-google-prompt-cache-tool-snapshots --shell -- "pnpm check:changed"` selected provider `azure` but failed before sync/lease allocation because sparse-sync full-checkout preflight reported insufficient disk at `732.4 MiB` free against its `1.00 GiB` minimum.

target.title: fix(install): trap SIGINT so Ctrl+C exits cleanly during upgrade doctor
target.body:
Fixes #90011

## Problem

When running `curl -fsSL https://openclaw.ai/install.sh | bash`, pressing Ctrl+C during `openclaw doctor` in the upgrade flow does not abort the installer. The SIGINT kills the doctor subprocess but the installer catches it as a regular failure, continues past the prompt, and opens a browser to an unconfigured dashboard URL.

## Fix

1. **Add SIGINT/SIGTERM trap**: `abort_install_int`/`abort_install_term` handlers clean up temp files, show a warning, and exit 130/143
2. **Propagate doctor exit code 130**: capture `openclaw doctor` exit code separately; if 130 (SIGINT), call `abort_install_int` instead of treating it as a regular failure
3. **Preserve signal exit codes through `run_quiet_step`**: the wrapper previously normalized all failures to exit 1, which swallowed SIGINT (130). Now returns signal exit codes (>128) so the doctor exit-130 check fires correctly, while preserving exit 1 for non-signal failures to maintain existing caller semantics across all 66+ installer call sites
4. **Clear dashboard flag on doctor failure**: when the interactive upgrade-doctor exits non-zero (Clack cancellation exit 1, SIGINT exit 130, or ordinary failure), clear `should_open_dashboard` so the installer does not launch a dead dashboard after an incomplete upgrade
5. **Propagate run_doctor exit code**: `run_doctor()` now returns non-zero when the doctor fails, so the non-interactive upgrade path correctly skips dashboard launch on failure
6. **Fix Clack cancel exit code**: `guardCancel` in `onboard-helpers.ts` called `runtime.exit(0)` when the user cancelled at a Clack prompt (Escape key), making the install script treat cancellation as success. Changed to `runtime.exit(1)` to match the `WizardCancelledError` pattern used by `clack-prompter.ts` and `onboard-interactive.ts`

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** Pressing Ctrl+C (SIGINT) during `openclaw doctor` in the upgrade flow does not abort the installer. The SIGINT kills the doctor subprocess but the installer treats it as a regular failure and continues.
- **Real environment tested:** macOS 15.5 (Apple Silicon), bash 5.x (Homebrew), Node v24.15.0, npm 11.14.1, OpenClaw main at commit 85df2e1f85 (rebased).
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

  Step 1. Sent SIGINT to the installer during the npm install phase of a real upgrade on macOS:
  ```bash
  set -m; (bash scripts/install.sh --no-onboard --no-prompt 2>&1) & PID=$!; sleep 2; kill -INT -- -$PID; wait $PID; echo "exit=$?"
  ```

  Step 2. Verified `run_quiet_step` preserves the child exit code and SIGINT detection in doctor paths:
  ```bash
  grep -n 'cmd_exit\|return.*cmd_exit' scripts/install.sh
  grep -n 'abort_install_int\|trap.*INT\|doctor_exit.*130' scripts/install.sh
  ```

- **Evidence after fix:** terminal output from the patched install script:

  SIGINT during the install phase triggers clean abort with exit 130:

  ```console
  $ set -m; (bash scripts/install.sh --no-onboard --no-prompt 2>&1) & PID=$!; sleep 2; kill -INT -- -$PID; wait $PID; echo "exit=$?"

  Preparing installer interface...

    OpenClaw Installer

  Detected: macos
  Existing OpenClaw installation detected, upgrading

  [1/3] Preparing environment
  Node.js v24.15.0 found
  Using Node.js runtime at /Users/user/.nvm/versions/node/v24.15.0/bin/node

  [2/3] Installing OpenClaw
  Git already installed
  Installing OpenClaw v2026.5.28
  Installing OpenClaw package

  ! Installation interrupted
  exit=130
  ```

  Exit code 130 = 128 + SIGINT (signal 2). Temp files cleaned up, no dashboard opened.

  `run_quiet_step` now preserves signal exit codes (>128) while returning 1 for non-signal failures, keeping existing semantics for all other installer steps:

  ```console
  $ grep -n 'cmd_exit\|return.*cmd_exit' scripts/install.sh
  513:    local cmd_exit=0
  520:        run_with_spinner "$title" bash -c "${cmd_quoted}>${log_quoted} 2>&1" || cmd_exit=$?
  529:        "$@" >"$log" 2>&1 || cmd_exit=$?
  546:    if (( cmd_exit > 128 )); then
  547:        return "$cmd_exit"
  549:    return 1
  ```

  Both doctor code paths detect exit 130, call `abort_install_int`, and clear the dashboard flag on any non-zero exit:

  ```console
  $ grep -n 'should_open_dashboard\|abort_install_int\|doctor_exit.*!=.*0\|return.*doctor_exit' scripts/install.sh
  35:abort_install_int() {
  47:trap abort_install_int INT
  2836:        abort_install_int
  2838:    if (( doctor_exit != 0 )); then
  2839:        return "$doctor_exit"
  3132:    local should_open_dashboard=false
  3304:                abort_install_int
  3308:            if (( doctor_exit != 0 )); then
  3309:                should_open_dashboard=false
  ```

  Line 3308-3309 is the key fix: when the upgrade-doctor exits non-zero (Clack cancellation exit 1 or any failure), `should_open_dashboard` is cleared so the installer does not launch a dead dashboard. Lines 2838-2839: `run_doctor()` now propagates non-zero exit to its caller so the non-interactive upgrade path also skips the dashboard.

  Before fix (upstream main): the script hangs after SIGINT because the signal is not trapped. On Clack cancellation (exit 1), the dashboard still launches because `should_open_dashboard` was set true before the doctor ran.

  The `guardCancel` fix is active in the production bundle:

  ```console
  $ grep -n 'runtime.exit' dist/onboard-helpers-BrYVS1OL.js
  30:runtime.exit(1);
  ```

  Before fix: `runtime.exit(0)` made the install script treat Clack prompt cancellation as success, continuing with plugin updates. After fix: `runtime.exit(1)` causes the install script to correctly detect the non-zero exit and skip plugin updates ("Doctor failed; skipping plugin updates"). This matches the `WizardCancelledError` pattern used by `clack-prompter.ts` which throws (caught by callers, exit code 1).

- **Observed result after fix:** SIGINT during any phase triggers `abort_install_int`, prints "Installation interrupted", and exits 130. No dashboard opens. Clack prompt cancellation (Escape key) now exits 1 instead of 0, and the installer clears `should_open_dashboard` so no dead dashboard launches after an incomplete upgrade. Non-interrupt doctor failures clear the dashboard flag and skip plugin updates. The `run_quiet_step` wrapper returns signal exit codes (>128) so callers distinguish SIGINT from ordinary errors, while returning 1 for non-signal failures to maintain existing caller semantics.
- **What was not tested:** Linux systems with different shell implementations (dash, ash). Live Clack prompt cancellation during interactive doctor (requires a running gateway with pending migrations). The fix was verified through dist grep showing `runtime.exit(1)` in the compiled `guardCancel` function.

target.title: fix(tts): sanitize summarized speech text
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #90364.

TTS summarization now cleans summary-model output before speech synthesis:

- reuses the shared assistant-visible text sanitizer
- removes complete and EOF-truncated echoed `<text_to_summarize>` prompt blocks
- strips only prompt-specific leading summarizer preambles while preserving valid first-person and user-request summary prose
- preserves usable summary content after colon/dash prompt preambles
- caps the sanitized summary at the requested speech target length

## Root Cause

`src/tts/tts-core.ts` joined `TextContent` blocks from `completeSimple` and returned that raw string as `summary.summary`. Auto-TTS then assigned that value directly to `textForAudio`, so reasoning tags, model control tokens, prompt echo, or echoed original text could be spoken.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Long-response TTS summaries no longer pass hidden reasoning/control scaffolding, echoed summarization prompt text, complete or truncated echoed `<text_to_summarize>` content, or control tokens through to the audio text candidate. The review feedback cases are also covered: legitimate first-person summary prose is preserved, valid summaries starting with `The user asked me to summarize ...` are preserved, usable content after colon-style prompt preambles is preserved, and truncated prompt blocks are stripped through EOF.

Real environment tested: Dedicated local macOS OpenClaw source checkout on branch `codex/fix-tts-summary-sanitization` at commit `4a089bbf91`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: `pnpm exec tsx /private/tmp/openclaw-tts-summary-proof.ts`

Evidence after fix: Terminal capture from the local OpenClaw source run:

```console
$ pnpm exec tsx /private/tmp/openclaw-tts-summary-proof.ts
OpenClaw TTS summary proof
summary="Concise audible summary."
outputLength=24
targetLength=120
leakedForbiddenTokens=none
audioText=Concise audible summary.
```

target.title: fix(install): harden stdin consumers to prevent pipe corruption in curl | bash
target.body:
Fixes #90008

## Problem

When the OpenClaw installer is piped via `curl | bash`, child processes spawned
under `gum spin` can inherit the script stream as stdin. Gum v0.17.0 sets the
wrapped command's stdin to `os.Stdin`, allowing npm or other subprocesses to
consume bytes from the installer, corrupting execution.

Additionally, fallback paths (when gum raw mode fails or gum is unavailable)
redirected stdin unconditionally from `/dev/null`, breaking interactive commands
like Homebrew installation prompts when the script is run directly.

## Fix

**P1: Redirect gum-wrapped command stdin.** The normal `gum spin` path now
redirects stdin from `/dev/null`, isolating child commands from the piped
script stream:

```bash
"$GUM" spin --spinner dot --title "$title" -- "$@" < /dev/null >"$gum_out" 2>"$gum_err"
```

**P2: Conditional stdin redirect for fallback paths.** All fallback paths
(gum raw-mode fallback, non-gum fallback, `run_quiet_step` shell-function
path) now check `needs_stdin_isolation` before redirecting stdin:

```bash
if needs_stdin_isolation; then
    "$@" < /dev/null
else
    "$@"
fi
```

`needs_stdin_isolation` checks stdin directly (`! -t 0`) and `NO_PROMPT`,
without checking stdout (`-t 1`). This ensures stdout redirection (e.g.
`install.sh > log.txt`) does not suppress interactive prompts when stdin
is a terminal.

**P2: Labeler changes removed.** The size-label best-effort handling has been
removed from this PR to keep it focused on installer stdin safety. It belongs
in a separate PR.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** When the installer is piped via `curl | bash`, child processes spawned under `gum spin` can inherit the script stream as stdin. Gum v0.17.0 sets the wrapped command's stdin to `os.Stdin`, allowing npm or other subprocesses to consume bytes from the installer, corrupting execution. The fallback stdin guard used `is_non_interactive_shell` which checks stdout (`-t 1`), so `install.sh | tee log.txt` (stdout-piped but stdin is terminal) incorrectly suppresses interactive prompts.
- **Real environment tested:** macOS 15.5 arm64, bash 5.x (Homebrew), Node v26.0.0, OpenClaw built from patched source (commit 809aaecaf9).
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

  Step 1. Verified the new `needs_stdin_isolation` function checks stdin (`-t 0`), not stdout:
  ```bash
  grep -n -A3 'needs_stdin_isolation()' scripts/install.sh
  ```

  Step 2. Verified all fallback paths use `needs_stdin_isolation`:
  ```bash
  grep -n 'needs_stdin_isolation' scripts/install.sh
  ```

  Step 3. Ran the focused stdin isolation test suite:
  ```bash
  node scripts/run-vitest.mjs test/scripts/install-sh.test.ts --reporter=verbose
  ```

- **Evidence after fix:** terminal output demonstrating real stdin isolation in piped mode:

  **Before fix (baseline without isolation):** piped data leaks to the child process:

  ```console
  $ echo "SENTINEL_DATA_SHOULD_NOT_LEAK" | bash -c 'read -t 1 line 2>/dev/null; if [ -n "$line" ]; then echo LEAKED:$line; else echo ISOLATED; fi'
  LEAKED:SENTINEL_DATA_SHOULD_NOT_LEAK
  ```

  **After fix:** `run_quiet_step` redirects stdin to `/dev/null`, so the sentinel data does NOT reach the child:

  ```console
  $ echo "SENTINEL_DATA_SHOULD_NOT_LEAK" | OPENCLAW_INSTALL_SH_NO_RUN=1 bash -c '
    source scripts/install.sh && GUM="" && run_quiet_step "test-stdin" bash -c \
      "read -t 1 line 2>/dev/null; if [ -n \"\$line\" ]; then echo LEAKED:\$line > /tmp/stdin-proof-marker; else echo ISOLATED > /tmp/stdin-proof-marker; fi"'
  · test-stdin

  $ cat /tmp/stdin-proof-marker
  ISOLATED
  ```

  The child process attempted `read` on stdin but got nothing (stdin was `/dev/null`), proving the piped sentinel data did not leak through `run_quiet_step`. This is the exact scenario that causes corruption in `curl | bash` mode: without the fix, npm or other subprocesses consume bytes from the installer script stream.

  The `needs_stdin_isolation` function checks stdin (`-t 0`) directly, not stdout:

  ```console
  $ grep -n -A3 'needs_stdin_isolation()' scripts/install.sh
  114:needs_stdin_isolation() {
  115-    [[ ! -t 0 ]] || [[ "${NO_PROMPT:-0}" == "1" ]]
  116-}
  ```

  All 4 fallback paths use the stdin-aware check:

  ```console
  $ grep -n 'needs_stdin_isolation' scripts/install.sh
  114:needs_stdin_isolation() {
  467:                if needs_stdin_isolation; then
  485:            if needs_stdin_isolation; then
  501:    if needs_stdin_isolation; then
  534:        if needs_stdin_isolation; then
  ```

  The regression test uses the same sentinel-based technique, feeding data on stdin and asserting the child cannot read it:

  ```console
  $ npx vitest run test/scripts/install-sh.test.ts --reporter=verbose 2>&1 | grep -E 'stdin|ISOLATED|SENTINEL'
  ✓ run_quiet_step redirects stdin to /dev/null in piped context
  ```

- **Observed result after fix:** In piped mode (`curl | bash`), `needs_stdin_isolation` returns true based on stdin (`-t 0`), and `run_quiet_step` redirects child stdin to `/dev/null`. The sentinel-based before/after proof above confirms: without the fix, sentinel data leaks to the child (`LEAKED:SENTINEL_DATA_SHOULD_NOT_LEAK`); with the fix, the child reads nothing (`ISOLATED`). The regression test uses the same technique: feeds `SENTINEL_DATA_SHOULD_NOT_LEAK` on stdin and asserts the child cannot read it, proving actual stdin isolation rather than just TTY detection.
- **What was not tested:** Production `curl -fsSL https://openclaw.ai/install.sh | bash` with a real download. Live gum v0.17.0 interaction in piped mode.

## Files changed

- `scripts/install.sh`: stdin redirect fixes
- `test/scripts/install-sh.test.ts`: updated test assertions
- `.github/workflows/labeler.yml`: reverted to upstream (changes removed from this PR)

target.title: feat: add tart provider for macOS VMs on Apple Silicon
target.body:
## Summary

Add a `tart` provider for running macOS VMs on Apple Silicon via [Cirrus Labs tart](https://github.com/cirruslabs/tart). This enables local macOS staging environments using Apple's native Virtualization.framework -- no Parallels license needed.

## How it works

- **Acquire**: `tart clone` from OCI base image, `tart set` CPU/memory/disk, `tart run --no-graphics`, poll `tart ip`, inject SSH key via `tart exec`, wait for SSH readiness
- **Resolve**: find running VM by `crabbox-` name prefix
- **List**: `tart list --format json` filtered by prefix
- **Release**: `tart stop` + `tart delete`
- **Cleanup**: garbage collect stale VMs past idle timeout
- **Doctor**: verify tart CLI and list capability
- **SSH key injection**: via `tart exec` (no sshpass, no credentials on argv)
- **Ready check**: macOS-safe `uname -s && test -d ~` (no crabbox-ready script needed)

## Proof

Full lifecycle proven on M4 Pro Mac (macOS 26.4.1) over Tailscale SSH, tart 2.32.1:

```
=== FRESH PROOF ===
OS     CPU Memory Disk DiskFormat Size   Display  State
darwin 4   8192   50   raw        36.222 1024x768 stopped
192.168.64.2
Darwin admins-Virtual-Machine.local 22.6.0 ARM64_VMAPPLE arm64
ProductName:  macOS
ProductVersion: 13.7.4
BuildVersion: 22H420
STOP_OK
DELETE_OK
=== DONE ===
```

## Configuration

CLI flags: `--tart-image`, `--tart-cpu`, `--tart-memory`, `--tart-disk`

YAML (`.crabbox.yaml`):
```yaml
tart:
  image: ghcr.io/cirruslabs/macos-ventura-base:latest
  user: admin
  cpus: 4
  memory: 8192
  disk: 50
```

Env vars: `CRABBOX_TART_IMAGE`, `CRABBOX_TART_USER`, `CRABBOX_TART_WORK_ROOT`, `CRABBOX_TART_CPUS`, `CRABBOX_TART_MEMORY`, `CRABBOX_TART_DISK`

## Not included (follow-up)

- Shared-directory mounts (`tart run --dir`) -- needs explicit host-mount config, deferred
- Checkpoint/fork support -- tracked in separate PR

## Files changed

- `internal/providers/tart/provider.go` -- provider registration, spec, capability
- `internal/providers/tart/backend.go` -- full SSHLeaseBackend lifecycle
- `internal/providers/tart/core.go` -- type aliases bridging core exports
- `internal/providers/tart/flags.go` -- CLI flag registration
- `internal/providers/tart/provider_test.go` -- 12 unit tests
- `internal/cli/config.go` -- TartConfig struct, file config, env var loading
- `internal/cli/providers_builtin_test.go` -- test provider registration
- `internal/providers/all/all.go` -- import registration

## Test plan

- [x] 12 provider unit tests pass
- [x] `go build` / `go vet` / `go test` pass (Go 1.26.4 darwin/arm64)
- [x] Real lifecycle proof on M4 Pro Mac: clone + set + run + ip + exec + stop + delete
- [x] SSH key injection via tart exec (no sshpass)
- [x] Resource flags applied before boot (tart set --cpu/--memory/--disk-size)
- [x] YAML + env var config wired

target.title: [daemon] Preserve gateway drain during supervised restarts
target.body:
## Summary

- Change generated systemd gateway units from `KillMode=control-group` to `KillMode=mixed` so systemd sends the initial stop signal to the gateway main process before final cgroup cleanup.
- Centralize the managed gateway service stop budget at `330s`, covering the gateway's default 300s restart drain window plus shutdown reserve.
- Apply the same stop budget to macOS LaunchAgent `ExitTimeOut` so launchd does not SIGKILL the gateway before the default restart drain can finish.
- Update daemon tests, service audit, and Linux service docs so non-`mixed` systemd units and stale supervisor stop budgets are flagged instead of left healthy.

## Root Cause / Evidence

This fixes a narrow restart lifecycle mismatch observed on a real systemd-user OpenClaw gateway while applying a Telegram agent config change.

Observed timeline from the user service journal:

- `2026-06-03 15:46:22 UTC`: gateway detected config changes for `agents.list`, bindings, and the new Telegram group.
- `2026-06-03 15:46:23 UTC`: channel reload was deferred because `4 operation(s), 2 reply(ies), 2 embedded run(s)` were still active.
- `2026-06-03 15:49:13 UTC`: the gateway tool requested a restart to apply the Clawtributor agent config.
- `2026-06-03 15:51:08 UTC`: systemd delivered `SIGTERM`; the gateway logged that it was draining `4 active task(s)` and `2 active embedded run(s)` with timeout `300000ms`.
- `2026-06-03 15:51:08 UTC`: active Codex app-server stdio clients closed before turn completion, surfacing `codex app-server client closed before turn completed`.
- `2026-06-03 15:51:15 UTC`: the gateway finished draining and exited cleanly for a full supervisor restart.
- `2026-06-03 15:51:26 UTC`: the replacement gateway was listening again.

Code evidence:

- `src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.ts` already gives normal restarts a 300s active-work drain budget.
- `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/runs.ts` waits for active embedded runs to release session locks during restarts.
- `extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.ts` converts a closed app-server client before terminal turn notification into the exact prompt error seen in the journal.
- Upstream Codex app-server stdio is single-client style: `--listen stdio://` is the default transport, EOF closes the connection, and app-server exits its accept loop when no stdio connections remain.
- The generated OpenClaw systemd unit previously used `KillMode=control-group` and `TimeoutStopSec=30`, so systemd could signal all child app-server processes in the gateway cgroup while the gateway itself was trying to drain them.
- The generated macOS LaunchAgent previously used `ExitTimeOut=20`, which is also shorter than the default 300s restart drain.

`KillMode=mixed` preserves cgroup cleanup: systemd sends `SIGTERM` to the main process first, then still uses final cgroup cleanup if the stop timeout expires. That matches the gateway's drain ownership better than sending the initial stop signal to every child process.

## Scope Notes

This draft intentionally keeps the fix bounded to the generated supervisor defaults and docs.

One remaining edge is intentionally left as maintainer follow-up: `gateway.reload.deferralTimeoutMs` can be configured above the default 300s drain. This PR aligns systemd and launchd with the default budget, but it does not make service rendering derive `TimeoutStopSec` / `ExitTimeOut` from runtime config. Doing that would require threading reload config into the daemon service render/install path and is a larger config-surface change than this evidence PR needs.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: planned supervised gateway restarts can interrupt active child-process stdio runtimes before the gateway's own restart drain completes.

Real environment tested: Linux systemd-user OpenClaw gateway running from this checkout with `openclaw-gateway.service`, active child `codex app-server --listen stdio://` processes in the gateway cgroup, and the live incident journal described above.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```sh
node --import tsx -e "import { buildSystemdUnit } from './src/daemon/systemd-unit.ts'; const unit = buildSystemdUnit({ description: 'OpenClaw Gateway', programArguments: ['/usr/bin/node', '/repo/dist/index.js', 'gateway'], environment: {} }); console.log(unit.match(/^(TimeoutStopSec|KillMode)=.*$/gm).join('\\n'));"
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/daemon/service-audit.test.ts src/daemon/systemd-unit.test.ts src/daemon/launchd.test.ts
node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json src/daemon/service-audit.ts src/daemon/service-audit.test.ts
node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --write --threads=1 src/daemon/service-audit.ts src/daemon/service-audit.test.ts
git diff --check
.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local
```

Evidence after fix:

```text
TimeoutStopSec=330
KillMode=mixed
```

Targeted tests passed:

```text
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  4 passed (4)
Test Files  2 passed (2)
Tests  131 passed | 14 skipped (145)
```

Observed result after fix: the generated systemd unit now gives the gateway enough stop budget for the existing 300s restart drain and sends the initial stop signal to the gateway main process before child-process cgroup cleanup. The generated LaunchAgent now gives launchd the same 330s stop budget before SIGKILL. The service audit now flags unset or non-`mixed` systemd `KillMode` values, stale systemd stop budgets via `TimeoutStopSec` / ordered `TimeoutSec`, and stale launchd `ExitTimeOut` values so existing units can be repaired to the new drain-preserving policy.

What was not tested: I did not reinstall this patched unit/plist on a live gateway or trigger a new live restart while active Codex turns were running. This draft intentionally includes the production timeline so maintainers can decide whether they want a broader E2E restart proof before review-ready status.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/daemon/service-audit.test.ts src/daemon/systemd-unit.test.ts src/daemon/launchd.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json src/daemon/service-audit.ts src/daemon/service-audit.test.ts`
- `node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --write --threads=1 src/daemon/service-audit.ts src/daemon/service-audit.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local` - clean, no accepted/actionable findings

## AI Assistance

This PR is AI-assisted. I checked the generated service configs, daemon tests, the real systemd journal timeline, launchd/systemd stop-timeout behavior, and the relevant Codex app-server stdio behavior before opening and updating the draft.

target.title: Add channel turn delivery and control-lane diagnostics
target.body:
## Summary

This PR adds a small, source-level Agentic Kernel spike for OpenClaw channel turns. The goal is not a rewrite: it makes Telegram/direct-message delivery and control-lane health observable and testable through existing runtime paths.

Key pieces:
- Adds turn event state modeling for received messages, turn lifecycle, delivery requirements, delivery sent/failed, and tool call/result causality.
- Wires optional turn event recording into the existing channel turn kernel.
- Adds control-lane priority helpers and tests for direct Telegram-like turns.
- Adds gateway stability and channel-turn latency diagnostics for health/status/doctor surfaces.
- Tightens stale task-flow audit handling for ended flows.
- Keeps ordinary private Telegram DMs on the keyed lane while allowing only direct control-style DMs and authorized abort/stop messages to bypass the inbound debounce key.
- Keeps the spike internals out of the public plugin SDK contract.

## Verification

Prepared and tested locally on the source branch:
- targeted Vitest runtime suite: green
- `pnpm tsgo:test`: green
- `pnpm lint:extensions:no-plugin-sdk-internal`: green
- `pnpm lint:plugins:plugin-sdk-subpaths-exported`: green
- `git diff --check`: green
- Strong secret-pattern scans over the PR file set and final local diff found no `ops_`, OpenAI-style API keys, GitHub tokens, private keys, or AWS access keys.

## Maintainer-facing diagnostics context

This PR intentionally adds two operator-visible runtime diagnostics fields:

- `runtimeControlLane`: concise control-lane health for direct-message responsiveness, active/queued work, stale TaskFlows, and channel-turn delivery signals.
- `runtimeRecommendations`: sanitized next-action guidance derived from runtime health, without raw message bodies, secrets, mail contents, or private payloads.

These fields are meant for `status`, `health`, `doctor`, and gateway-health style surfaces so an operator can distinguish "agent is slow because work is queued", "direct control lane may be degraded", and "delivery proof is missing" without reading private chat history or patching runtime state. They are additive diagnostics; they do not change plugin SDK contracts or create a new public capability registry.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Telegram/direct-message control-lane and delivery diagnostics for Mira/OpenClaw must be observable from a real setup, with ordinary private Telegram DMs kept on a keyed lane and only direct control/abort messages allowed to bypass debounce.
- **Real environment tested**: Sebastian's local Mira/OpenClaw runtime on the Mac-node setup, with Telegram connected, cron/tasks visible, and the real `mira-main` node path available. The evidence below is redacted and contains no message bodies, reminder titles, reminder bodies, or secret values.

target.title: fix(install): trap SIGINT so Ctrl+C exits cleanly during upgrade doctor
target.body:
Fixes #90011

## Problem

When running `curl -fsSL https://openclaw.ai/install.sh | bash`, pressing Ctrl+C during `openclaw doctor` in the upgrade flow does not abort the installer. The SIGINT kills the doctor subprocess but the installer catches it as a regular failure, continues past the prompt, and opens a browser to an unconfigured dashboard URL.

## Fix

1. **Add SIGINT/SIGTERM trap**: `abort_install_int`/`abort_install_term` handlers clean up temp files, show a warning, and exit 130/143
2. **Propagate doctor exit code 130**: capture `openclaw doctor` exit code separately; if 130 (SIGINT), call `abort_install_int` instead of treating it as a regular failure
3. **Preserve signal exit codes through `run_quiet_step`**: the wrapper previously normalized all failures to exit 1, which swallowed SIGINT (130). Now returns signal exit codes (>128) so the doctor exit-130 check fires correctly, while preserving exit 1 for non-signal failures to maintain existing caller semantics across all 66+ installer call sites
4. **Clear dashboard flag on doctor failure**: when the interactive upgrade-doctor exits non-zero (Clack cancellation exit 1, SIGINT exit 130, or ordinary failure), clear `should_open_dashboard` so the installer does not launch a dead dashboard after an incomplete upgrade
5. **Propagate run_doctor exit code**: `run_doctor()` now returns non-zero when the doctor fails, so the non-interactive upgrade path correctly skips dashboard launch on failure
6. **Fix Clack cancel exit code**: `guardCancel` in `onboard-helpers.ts` called `runtime.exit(0)` when the user cancelled at a Clack prompt (Escape key), making the install script treat cancellation as success. Changed to `runtime.exit(1)` to match the `WizardCancelledError` pattern used by `clack-prompter.ts` and `onboard-interactive.ts`

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** Pressing Ctrl+C (SIGINT) during `openclaw doctor` in the upgrade flow does not abort the installer. The SIGINT kills the doctor subprocess but the installer treats it as a regular failure and continues.
- **Real environment tested:** macOS 15.5 (Apple Silicon), bash 5.x (Homebrew), Node v24.15.0, npm 11.14.1, OpenClaw main at commit 85df2e1f85 (rebased).
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

  Step 1. Sent SIGINT to the installer during the npm install phase of a real upgrade on macOS:
  ```bash
  set -m; (bash scripts/install.sh --no-onboard --no-prompt 2>&1) & PID=$!; sleep 2; kill -INT -- -$PID; wait $PID; echo "exit=$?"
  ```

  Step 2. Verified `run_quiet_step` preserves the child exit code and SIGINT detection in doctor paths:
  ```bash
  grep -n 'cmd_exit\|return.*cmd_exit' scripts/install.sh
  grep -n 'abort_install_int\|trap.*INT\|doctor_exit.*130' scripts/install.sh
  ```

- **Evidence after fix:** terminal output from the patched install script:

  SIGINT during the install phase triggers clean abort with exit 130:

  ```console
  $ set -m; (bash scripts/install.sh --no-onboard --no-prompt 2>&1) & PID=$!; sleep 2; kill -INT -- -$PID; wait $PID; echo "exit=$?"

  Preparing installer interface...

    OpenClaw Installer

  Detected: macos
  Existing OpenClaw installation detected, upgrading

  [1/3] Preparing environment
  Node.js v24.15.0 found
  Using Node.js runtime at /Users/user/.nvm/versions/node/v24.15.0/bin/node

  [2/3] Installing OpenClaw
  Git already installed
  Installing OpenClaw v2026.5.28
  Installing OpenClaw package

  ! Installation interrupted
  exit=130
  ```

  Exit code 130 = 128 + SIGINT (signal 2). Temp files cleaned up, no dashboard opened.

  `run_quiet_step` now preserves signal exit codes (>128) while returning 1 for non-signal failures, keeping existing semantics for all other installer steps:

  ```console
  $ grep -n 'cmd_exit\|return.*cmd_exit' scripts/install.sh
  513:    local cmd_exit=0
  520:        run_with_spinner "$title" bash -c "${cmd_quoted}>${log_quoted} 2>&1" || cmd_exit=$?
  529:        "$@" >"$log" 2>&1 || cmd_exit=$?
  546:    if (( cmd_exit > 128 )); then
  547:        return "$cmd_exit"
  549:    return 1
  ```

  Both doctor code paths detect exit 130, call `abort_install_int`, and clear the dashboard flag on any non-zero exit:

  ```console
  $ grep -n 'should_open_dashboard\|abort_install_int\|doctor_exit.*!=.*0\|return.*doctor_exit' scripts/install.sh
  35:abort_install_int() {
  47:trap abort_install_int INT
  2836:        abort_install_int
  2838:    if (( doctor_exit != 0 )); then
  2839:        return "$doctor_exit"
  3132:    local should_open_dashboard=false
  3304:                abort_install_int
  3308:            if (( doctor_exit != 0 )); then
  3309:                should_open_dashboard=false
  ```

  Line 3308-3309 is the key fix: when the upgrade-doctor exits non-zero (Clack cancellation exit 1 or any failure), `should_open_dashboard` is cleared so the installer does not launch a dead dashboard. Lines 2838-2839: `run_doctor()` now propagates non-zero exit to its caller so the non-interactive upgrade path also skips the dashboard.

  Before fix (upstream main): the script hangs after SIGINT because the signal is not trapped. On Clack cancellation (exit 1), the dashboard still launches because `should_open_dashboard` was set true before the doctor ran.

  The `guardCancel` fix is active in the production bundle:

  ```console
  $ grep -n 'runtime.exit' dist/onboard-helpers-BrYVS1OL.js
  30:runtime.exit(1);
  ```

  Before fix: `runtime.exit(0)` made the install script treat Clack prompt cancellation as success, continuing with plugin updates. After fix: `runtime.exit(1)` causes the install script to correctly detect the non-zero exit and skip plugin updates ("Doctor failed; skipping plugin updates"). This matches the `WizardCancelledError` pattern used by `clack-prompter.ts` which throws (caught by callers, exit code 1).

- **Observed result after fix:** SIGINT during any phase triggers `abort_install_int`, prints "Installation interrupted", and exits 130. No dashboard opens. Clack prompt cancellation (Escape key) now exits 1 instead of 0, and the installer clears `should_open_dashboard` so no dead dashboard launches after an incomplete upgrade. Non-interrupt doctor failures clear the dashboard flag and skip plugin updates. The `run_quiet_step` wrapper returns signal exit codes (>128) so callers distinguish SIGINT from ordinary errors, while returning 1 for non-signal failures to maintain existing caller semantics.
- **What was not tested:** Linux systems with different shell implementations (dash, ash). Live Clack prompt cancellation during interactive doctor (requires a running gateway with pending migrations). The fix was verified through dist grep showing `runtime.exit(1)` in the compiled `guardCancel` function.

target.title: openai-chatgpt-responses native replay sends encrypted reasoning and breaks next turn with invalid_encrypted_content
target.body:
## Summary

On OpenClaw 2026.6.1, a native/OpenClaw-runtime `openai-chatgpt-responses` session can succeed on the first turn and then fail on the next turn with a provider-side `400 invalid_encrypted_content` before any model output.

The user-visible error is sanitized to:

```text
LLM request failed: provider rejected the request schema or tool payload.
```

The raw trajectory error is:

```text
Codex error: {"type":"error","error":{"type":"invalid_request_error","code":"invalid_encrypted_content","message":"The encrypted content gAAA... could not be verified. Reason: Encrypted content could not be decrypted or parsed."},"status":400}
```

## Environment

- OpenClaw: `2026.6.1` (`2e08f0f`)
- macOS: `26.4.1` arm64
- Node: `25.9.0`
- Runtime / harness: OpenClaw native (`agentRuntime.id = "openclaw"`)
- Provider/model shape:

```json
{
  "provider": "openai",
  "model": "gpt-5.5",
  "api": "openai-chatgpt-responses",
  "baseUrl": "https://chatgpt.com/backend-api/codex",
  "agentRuntime": { "id": "openclaw" },
  "params": { "thinking": "xhigh", "transport": "sse" }
}
```

## Reproduction observed

1. Start a fresh session using `openai/gpt-5.5` with `api=openai-chatgpt-responses` and native runtime.
2. Send a first turn that causes a normal model response and tool use. This succeeds.
3. The successful assistant turn stores a `thinking` block with `thinkingSignature` containing a serialized Responses reasoning item with `encrypted_content`.
4. Send a second turn in the same session.
5. OpenClaw replays the prior assistant reasoning item in the submitted history.
6. The ChatGPT/Codex backend rejects the request before model output with `400 invalid_encrypted_content`.

This explains why the previous turn succeeds but the next turn fails.

## What I found in dist

`openai-chatgpt-responses` requests encrypted reasoning:

```js
// openai-chatgpt-responses-*.js
include: ["reasoning.encrypted_content"]
```

`sanitizeSessionHistory` only drops reasoning history when the provider replay policy says so:

```js
const droppedReasoning = policy.dropReasoningFromHistory
  ? dropReasoningFromHistory(validatedThinkingSignatures)
  : validatedThinkingSignatures;
```

But `buildOpenAIReplayPolicy(ctx)` currently treats `openai-chatgpt-responses` as a Responses-family API with synthetic tool results, and does not set `dropReasoningFromHistory` for this API.

## Local mitigation that fixed it

Adding this to `buildOpenAIReplayPolicy(ctx)` fixed the same session immediately:

```js
...ctx.modelApi === "openai-chatgpt-responses" ? { dropReasoningFromHistory: true } : {},
```

After restarting the gateway, the same session and same message completed successfully. The submitted history no longer contained `thinkingSignature`; only the old failed assistant turn's plain-text `errorMessage` still contained the string `invalid_encrypted_content`, which is harmless.

## Expected behavior

For `openai-chatgpt-responses` against the ChatGPT/Codex backend, OpenClaw should not replay stale/unverifiable encrypted reasoning content, or should retry safely without it before surfacing an error.

## Actual behavior

The next turn replays encrypted reasoning content and the backend rejects the whole request with `400 invalid_encrypted_content`, resulting in an empty/non-deliverable assistant turn.

## Related

- Related to #87151 (`openai-codex` native Responses encrypted reasoning replay)
- Related to #88917 (stale Responses reasoning replay retry)
- Related to #84511 (closed, OAuth refresh variant)
- Also observed while testing the native/Pi route discussed in #90086, but this issue is specifically about encrypted reasoning replay.

target.title: fix(qa-lab): prevent child process signal bleeding under Node 24 (#90…
target.body: Fixes a regression under Node 24 on WSL2 where background tool execution triggers a full gateway restart (closes #90428). In `gateway-child.ts`, using `process.kill(-child.pid)` broadcasts signals to the entire process group, causing `SIGTERM` signals to bleed backward into the parent gateway process when managed within a shared `systemd --user` slice context. This patch mitigates the signal bleeding by adding a `child.detached` safety check, ensuring group-wide signaling is only executed if the child is isolated, and falling back safely to a targeted `child.kill(signal)` if the process group context is shared.

target.title: fix(telegram): suppress reconnect drain re-entry while delivery is in-flight
target.body:
## Summary

When a live Telegram send stalls (e.g. 129 seconds as reported in #89953), every subsequent `poll-success` event triggers a reconnect drain that finds the same pending delivery already claimed by the in-flight send. The drain returns immediately, clears the `#deliveryDrainInFlight` guard, and the next poll re-triggers the cycle. This produces repeated "already being recovered" log entries every ~30 seconds until the stalled send completes.

## Root cause

Two separate in-flight tracking mechanisms interact:

1. `#deliveryDrainInFlight` in `TelegramPollingSession`: prevents concurrent drain calls, but resets as soon as the drain function returns.
2. `entriesInProgress` in `delivery-queue-recovery.ts`: tracks individual entries claimed by live sends via `withActiveDeliveryClaim`.

When the live send holds an entry via `withActiveDeliveryClaim` (in `deliver.ts`), the drain function quickly skips the entry ("already being recovered"), returns, and clears the session-level guard. The next poll success starts a fresh drain that again finds the same claimed entry.

## Fix

1. **`delivery-queue-recovery.ts`**: `drainPendingDeliveries` now returns a `ReconnectDrainResult` with `{ matched, drained, skippedInProgress }` counts instead of `void`.

2. **`delivery-queue-runtime.ts` (Plugin SDK)**: The public `drainPendingDeliveries` export preserves its existing `Promise<void>` return contract. A new additive `drainPendingDeliveriesWithResult` function returns `Promise<ReconnectDrainResult>` for callers that need drain counts.

3. **`polling-session.ts`**: Uses `drainPendingDeliveriesWithResult` to inspect drain results. When `skippedInProgress > 0 && drained === 0`, sets a 60-second cooldown (two Telegram long-poll intervals) before the next drain attempt. The wider cooldown ensures a delayed poll-success arriving after one normal 30s interval cannot re-enter the same drain.

This eliminates the repeated log noise and wasted I/O from futile drain attempts while preserving the ability to drain genuinely new entries as soon as the cooldown expires or in-flight sends complete.

## Changed files

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/infra/outbound/delivery-queue-recovery.ts` | Add `ReconnectDrainResult` type; track `skippedInProgress`/`drained` counters in `drainPendingDeliveries`; return the result |
| `src/infra/outbound/delivery-queue.ts` | Re-export `ReconnectDrainResult` type |
| `src/plugin-sdk/delivery-queue-runtime.ts` | Keep `drainPendingDeliveries` as `Promise<void>` (preserves SDK contract); add `drainPendingDeliveriesWithResult` returning `Promise<ReconnectDrainResult>` |
| `extensions/telegram/src/polling-session.ts` | Import `drainPendingDeliveriesWithResult`; add 60s drain cooldown; check cooldown in `#drainPendingDeliveriesAfterReconnect`; set cooldown when all entries are in-progress |

## Tests

- 3 new tests in `delivery-queue.reconnect-drain.test.ts`: verify drain result reports `skippedInProgress` for claimed entries, `drained` for recovered entries, and empty result for no matches
- 6 tests in `polling-session.test.ts` covering drain cooldown: re-entry suppressed when all in-progress, still suppressed after one long-poll interval (delayed poll-success regression at 35s), suppressed past full HTTP request timeout and expires after cooldown (delayed regression at 46s/61s), not suppressed when entries drained, not suppressed when some were drained (mixed result)
- All 58 polling-session + 3 delivery-queue reconnect-drain tests pass

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Telegram reconnect drain re-enters on every poll-success (~30s interval) while a live send holds the pending delivery via `withActiveDeliveryClaim`, producing repeated "already being recovered" log messages and wasted I/O until the stall resolves (129s observed in #89953).
- **Real environment tested:** macOS 15.5 (darwin 25.5.0), Node v26.0.0, OpenClaw main at commit 85df2e1f85 (rebased), pnpm 11.2.2.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

  Step 1. Built from the patched branch:
  ```bash
  pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
  pnpm build
  ```

  Step 2. Verified cooldown, SDK contract preservation, and drain tracking in production bundle:
  ```bash
  grep -n "DELIVERY_DRAIN_COOLDOWN_MS\|deliveryDrainCooldownUntil\|skippedInProgress" dist/monitor-polling.runtime-DLFq4M1O.js
  grep -n "drainPendingDeliveries\b\|drainPendingDeliveriesWithResult" dist/delivery-queue-runtime-Dv-0a9G_.js
  ```

  Step 3. Ran the polling session test suite to verify behavioral correctness:
  ```bash
  node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/polling-session.test.ts --reporter=verbose
  ```

- **Evidence after fix:** terminal output from the patched build:

  The 60-second cooldown guard is active in the production Telegram polling runtime:

  ```console
  $ grep -n "DELIVERY_DRAIN_COOLDOWN_MS\|deliveryDrainCooldownUntil\|skippedInProgress" dist/monitor-polling.runtime-DLFq4M1O.js
  476:const DELIVERY_DRAIN_COOLDOWN_MS = 6e4;
  552:#deliveryDrainCooldownUntil = 0;
  618:if (Date.now() < this.#deliveryDrainCooldownUntil) return;
  637:if (drainResult.skippedInProgress > 0 && drainResult.drained === 0) this.#deliveryDrainCooldownUntil = Date.now() + DELIVERY_DRAIN_COOLDOWN_MS;
  ```

  The public SDK preserves the `Promise<void>` contract via `drainPendingDeliveries`, while the new `drainPendingDeliveriesWithResult` (additive export) returns drain counts for internal callers:

  ```console
  $ grep -n "drainPendingDeliveries\b\|drainPendingDeliveriesWithResult" dist/delivery-queue-runtime-Dv-0a9G_.js
  13:async function drainPendingDeliveries(opts) {
  14:await drainPendingDeliveriesWithResult(opts);
  22:async function drainPendingDeliveriesWithResult(opts) {
  30:export { drainPendingDeliveriesWithResult as n, drainPendingDeliveries as t };
  ```

  The `@since` annotation documents `drainPendingDeliveriesWithResult` as additive:
  ```console
  $ grep -A2 "Additive export" dist/delivery-queue-runtime-Dv-0a9G_.js
  20:* Additive export; the existing {@link drainPendingDeliveries} void contract is unchanged.
  ```

  Terminal confirms the behavioral change via the polling session test suite:

  ```console
  ✓ suppresses reconnect drain re-entry when all entries are already being recovered
  ✓ still suppresses drain after one long-poll interval when entries remain in-flight
  ✓ suppresses drain past the full HTTP request timeout and expires after the cooldown
  ✓ does not suppress drain when entries were actually drained
  ✓ does not suppress drain when some entries were successfully drained
  ```

  The delayed regression tests verify the 60s cooldown covers the worst case: the 35s test covers one normal long-poll interval, and the 46s test covers the full HTTP request timeout (45s). The 61s test verifies the cooldown correctly expires and allows new drains.

- **Observed result after fix:** The compiled production code at `dist/monitor-polling.runtime-DLFq4M1O.js:476` uses a 60-second cooldown (6e4 ms), covering the full Telegram HTTP request timeout (45s) with 15s of margin. Line 618 short-circuits drain attempts during the cooldown window. Line 637 activates cooldown only when `skippedInProgress > 0 && drained === 0`, so normal drains that successfully recover entries are unaffected. The SDK contract at `dist/delivery-queue-runtime-Dv-0a9G_.js:13` preserves `drainPendingDeliveries` as `Promise<void>` for backward compatibility while exposing the additive result-bearing variant at line 22. The delayed regression test at 46s proves the cooldown survives past the HTTP request timeout, and the test at 61s proves the cooldown correctly expires.
- **What was not tested:** Live Telegram send stall scenario requiring a real Telegram bot with network conditions that cause 129s+ stalls. The cooldown duration (60s) covers two long-poll intervals to handle delayed poll-success; production tuning may adjust it.

Closes #89953

target.title: fix(install): trap SIGINT so Ctrl+C exits cleanly during upgrade doctor
target.body:
Fixes #90011

## Problem

When running `curl -fsSL https://openclaw.ai/install.sh | bash`, pressing Ctrl+C during `openclaw doctor` in the upgrade flow does not abort the installer. The SIGINT kills the doctor subprocess but the installer catches it as a regular failure, continues past the prompt, and opens a browser to an unconfigured dashboard URL.

## Fix

1. **Add SIGINT/SIGTERM trap**: `abort_install_int`/`abort_install_term` handlers clean up temp files, show a warning, and exit 130/143
2. **Propagate doctor exit code 130**: capture `openclaw doctor` exit code separately; if 130 (SIGINT), call `abort_install_int` instead of treating it as a regular failure
3. **Preserve signal exit codes through `run_quiet_step`**: the wrapper previously normalized all failures to exit 1, which swallowed SIGINT (130). Now returns signal exit codes (>128) so the doctor exit-130 check fires correctly, while preserving exit 1 for non-signal failures to maintain existing caller semantics across all 66+ installer call sites
4. **Clear dashboard flag on doctor failure**: when the interactive upgrade-doctor exits non-zero (Clack cancellation exit 1, SIGINT exit 130, or ordinary failure), clear `should_open_dashboard` so the installer does not launch a dead dashboard after an incomplete upgrade
5. **Propagate run_doctor exit code**: `run_doctor()` now returns non-zero when the doctor fails, so the non-interactive upgrade path correctly skips dashboard launch on failure
6. **Fix Clack cancel exit code**: `guardCancel` in `onboard-helpers.ts` called `runtime.exit(0)` when the user cancelled at a Clack prompt (Escape key), making the install script treat cancellation as success. Changed to `runtime.exit(1)` to match the `WizardCancelledError` pattern used by `clack-prompter.ts` and `onboard-interactive.ts`

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** Pressing Ctrl+C (SIGINT) during `openclaw doctor` in the upgrade flow does not abort the installer. The SIGINT kills the doctor subprocess but the installer treats it as a regular failure and continues.
- **Real environment tested:** macOS 15.5 (Apple Silicon), bash 5.x (Homebrew), Node v24.15.0, npm 11.14.1, OpenClaw main at commit 85df2e1f85 (rebased).
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

  Step 1. Sent SIGINT to the installer during the npm install phase of a real upgrade on macOS:
  ```bash
  set -m; (bash scripts/install.sh --no-onboard --no-prompt 2>&1) & PID=$!; sleep 2; kill -INT -- -$PID; wait $PID; echo "exit=$?"
  ```

  Step 2. Verified `run_quiet_step` preserves the child exit code and SIGINT detection in doctor paths:
  ```bash
  grep -n 'cmd_exit\|return.*cmd_exit' scripts/install.sh
  grep -n 'abort_install_int\|trap.*INT\|doctor_exit.*130' scripts/install.sh
  ```

- **Evidence after fix:** terminal output from the patched install script:

  SIGINT during the install phase triggers clean abort with exit 130:

  ```console
  $ set -m; (bash scripts/install.sh --no-onboard --no-prompt 2>&1) & PID=$!; sleep 2; kill -INT -- -$PID; wait $PID; echo "exit=$?"

  Preparing installer interface...

    OpenClaw Installer

  Detected: macos
  Existing OpenClaw installation detected, upgrading

  [1/3] Preparing environment
  Node.js v24.15.0 found
  Using Node.js runtime at /Users/user/.nvm/versions/node/v24.15.0/bin/node

  [2/3] Installing OpenClaw
  Git already installed
  Installing OpenClaw v2026.5.28
  Installing OpenClaw package

  ! Installation interrupted
  exit=130
  ```

  Exit code 130 = 128 + SIGINT (signal 2). Temp files cleaned up, no dashboard opened.

  `run_quiet_step` now preserves signal exit codes (>128) while returning 1 for non-signal failures, keeping existing semantics for all other installer steps:

  ```console
  $ grep -n 'cmd_exit\|return.*cmd_exit' scripts/install.sh
  513:    local cmd_exit=0
  520:        run_with_spinner "$title" bash -c "${cmd_quoted}>${log_quoted} 2>&1" || cmd_exit=$?
  529:        "$@" >"$log" 2>&1 || cmd_exit=$?
  546:    if (( cmd_exit > 128 )); then
  547:        return "$cmd_exit"
  549:    return 1
  ```

  Both doctor code paths detect exit 130, call `abort_install_int`, and clear the dashboard flag on any non-zero exit:

  ```console
  $ grep -n 'should_open_dashboard\|abort_install_int\|doctor_exit.*!=.*0\|return.*doctor_exit' scripts/install.sh
  35:abort_install_int() {
  47:trap abort_install_int INT
  2836:        abort_install_int
  2838:    if (( doctor_exit != 0 )); then
  2839:        return "$doctor_exit"
  3132:    local should_open_dashboard=false
  3304:                abort_install_int
  3308:            if (( doctor_exit != 0 )); then
  3309:                should_open_dashboard=false
  ```

  Line 3308-3309 is the key fix: when the upgrade-doctor exits non-zero (Clack cancellation exit 1 or any failure), `should_open_dashboard` is cleared so the installer does not launch a dead dashboard. Lines 2838-2839: `run_doctor()` now propagates non-zero exit to its caller so the non-interactive upgrade path also skips the dashboard.

  Before fix (upstream main): the script hangs after SIGINT because the signal is not trapped. On Clack cancellation (exit 1), the dashboard still launches because `should_open_dashboard` was set true before the doctor ran.

  The `guardCancel` fix is active in the production bundle:

  ```console
  $ grep -n 'runtime.exit' dist/onboard-helpers-BrYVS1OL.js
  30:runtime.exit(1);
  ```

  Before fix: `runtime.exit(0)` made the install script treat Clack prompt cancellation as success, continuing with plugin updates. After fix: `runtime.exit(1)` causes the install script to correctly detect the non-zero exit and skip plugin updates ("Doctor failed; skipping plugin updates"). This matches the `WizardCancelledError` pattern used by `clack-prompter.ts` which throws (caught by callers, exit code 1).

- **Observed result after fix:** SIGINT during any phase triggers `abort_install_int`, prints "Installation interrupted", and exits 130. No dashboard opens. Clack prompt cancellation (Escape key) now exits 1 instead of 0, and the installer clears `should_open_dashboard` so no dead dashboard launches after an incomplete upgrade. Non-interrupt doctor failures clear the dashboard flag and skip plugin updates. The `run_quiet_step` wrapper returns signal exit codes (>128) so callers distinguish SIGINT from ordinary errors, while returning 1 for non-signal failures to maintain existing caller semantics.
- **What was not tested:** Linux systems with different shell implementations (dash, ash). Live Clack prompt cancellation during interactive doctor (requires a running gateway with pending migrations). The fix was verified through dist grep showing `runtime.exit(1)` in the compiled `guardCancel` function.

target.body:
## Summary

When a live Telegram send stalls (e.g. 129 seconds as reported in #89953), every subsequent `poll-success` event triggers a reconnect drain that finds the same pending delivery already claimed by the in-flight send. The drain returns immediately, clears the `#deliveryDrainInFlight` guard, and the next poll re-triggers the cycle. This produces repeated "already being recovered" log entries every ~30 seconds until the stalled send completes.

## Root cause

Two separate in-flight tracking mechanisms interact:

1. `#deliveryDrainInFlight` in `TelegramPollingSession`: prevents concurrent drain calls, but resets as soon as the drain function returns.
2. `entriesInProgress` in `delivery-queue-recovery.ts`: tracks individual entries claimed by live sends via `withActiveDeliveryClaim`.

When the live send holds an entry via `withActiveDeliveryClaim` (in `deliver.ts`), the drain function quickly skips the entry ("already being recovered"), returns, and clears the session-level guard. The next poll success starts a fresh drain that again finds the same claimed entry.

## Fix

1. **`delivery-queue-recovery.ts`**: `drainPendingDeliveries` now returns a `ReconnectDrainResult` with `{ matched, drained, skippedInProgress }` counts instead of `void`.

2. **`delivery-queue-runtime.ts` (Plugin SDK)**: The public `drainPendingDeliveries` export preserves its existing `Promise<void>` return contract. A new additive `drainPendingDeliveriesWithResult` function returns `Promise<ReconnectDrainResult>` for callers that need drain counts.

3. **`polling-session.ts`**: Uses `drainPendingDeliveriesWithResult` to inspect drain results. When `skippedInProgress > 0 && drained === 0`, sets a 60-second cooldown (two Telegram long-poll intervals) before the next drain attempt. The wider cooldown ensures a delayed poll-success arriving after one normal 30s interval cannot re-enter the same drain.

This eliminates the repeated log noise and wasted I/O from futile drain attempts while preserving the ability to drain genuinely new entries as soon as the cooldown expires or in-flight sends complete.

## Changed files

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/infra/outbound/delivery-queue-recovery.ts` | Add `ReconnectDrainResult` type; track `skippedInProgress`/`drained` counters in `drainPendingDeliveries`; return the result |
| `src/infra/outbound/delivery-queue.ts` | Re-export `ReconnectDrainResult` type |
| `src/plugin-sdk/delivery-queue-runtime.ts` | Keep `drainPendingDeliveries` as `Promise<void>` (preserves SDK contract); add `drainPendingDeliveriesWithResult` returning `Promise<ReconnectDrainResult>` |
| `extensions/telegram/src/polling-session.ts` | Import `drainPendingDeliveriesWithResult`; add 60s drain cooldown; check cooldown in `#drainPendingDeliveriesAfterReconnect`; set cooldown when all entries are in-progress |

## Tests

- 3 new tests in `delivery-queue.reconnect-drain.test.ts`: verify drain result reports `skippedInProgress` for claimed entries, `drained` for recovered entries, and empty result for no matches
- 6 tests in `polling-session.test.ts` covering drain cooldown: re-entry suppressed when all in-progress, still suppressed after one long-poll interval (delayed poll-success regression at 35s), suppressed past full HTTP request timeout and expires after cooldown (delayed regression at 46s/61s), not suppressed when entries drained, not suppressed when some were drained (mixed result)
- All 58 polling-session + 3 delivery-queue reconnect-drain tests pass

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Telegram reconnect drain re-enters on every poll-success (~30s interval) while a live send holds the pending delivery via `withActiveDeliveryClaim`, producing repeated "already being recovered" log messages and wasted I/O until the stall resolves (129s observed in #89953).
- **Real environment tested:** macOS 15.5 (darwin 25.5.0), Node v26.0.0, OpenClaw main at commit 85df2e1f85 (rebased), pnpm 11.2.2.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

  Step 1. Built from the patched branch:
  ```bash
  pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
  pnpm build
  ```

  Step 2. Verified cooldown, SDK contract preservation, and drain tracking in production bundle:
  ```bash
  grep -n "DELIVERY_DRAIN_COOLDOWN_MS\|deliveryDrainCooldownUntil\|skippedInProgress" dist/monitor-polling.runtime-DLFq4M1O.js
  grep -n "drainPendingDeliveries\b\|drainPendingDeliveriesWithResult" dist/delivery-queue-runtime-Dv-0a9G_.js
  ```

  Step 3. Ran the polling session test suite to verify behavioral correctness:
  ```bash
  node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/polling-session.test.ts --reporter=verbose
  ```

- **Evidence after fix:** terminal output from the patched build:

  The 60-second cooldown guard is active in the production Telegram polling runtime:

  ```console
  $ grep -n "DELIVERY_DRAIN_COOLDOWN_MS\|deliveryDrainCooldownUntil\|skippedInProgress" dist/monitor-polling.runtime-DLFq4M1O.js
  476:const DELIVERY_DRAIN_COOLDOWN_MS = 6e4;
  552:#deliveryDrainCooldownUntil = 0;
  618:if (Date.now() < this.#deliveryDrainCooldownUntil) return;
  637:if (drainResult.skippedInProgress > 0 && drainResult.drained === 0) this.#deliveryDrainCooldownUntil = Date.now() + DELIVERY_DRAIN_COOLDOWN_MS;
  ```

  The public SDK preserves the `Promise<void>` contract via `drainPendingDeliveries`, while the new `drainPendingDeliveriesWithResult` (additive export) returns drain counts for internal callers:

  ```console
  $ grep -n "drainPendingDeliveries\b\|drainPendingDeliveriesWithResult" dist/delivery-queue-runtime-Dv-0a9G_.js
  13:async function drainPendingDeliveries(opts) {
  14:await drainPendingDeliveriesWithResult(opts);
  22:async function drainPendingDeliveriesWithResult(opts) {
  30:export { drainPendingDeliveriesWithResult as n, drainPendingDeliveries as t };
  ```

  The `@since` annotation documents `drainPendingDeliveriesWithResult` as additive:
  ```console
  $ grep -A2 "Additive export" dist/delivery-queue-runtime-Dv-0a9G_.js
  20:* Additive export; the existing {@link drainPendingDeliveries} void contract is unchanged.
  ```

  Terminal confirms the behavioral change via the polling session test suite:

  ```console
  ✓ suppresses reconnect drain re-entry when all entries are already being recovered
  ✓ still suppresses drain after one long-poll interval when entries remain in-flight
  ✓ suppresses drain past the full HTTP request timeout and expires after the cooldown
  ✓ does not suppress drain when entries were actually drained
  ✓ does not suppress drain when some entries were successfully drained
  ```

  The delayed regression tests verify the 60s cooldown covers the worst case: the 35s test covers one normal long-poll interval, and the 46s test covers the full HTTP request timeout (45s). The 61s test verifies the cooldown correctly expires and allows new drains.

- **Observed result after fix:** The compiled production code at `dist/monitor-polling.runtime-DLFq4M1O.js:476` uses a 60-second cooldown (6e4 ms), covering the full Telegram HTTP request timeout (45s) with 15s of margin. Line 618 short-circuits drain attempts during the cooldown window. Line 637 activates cooldown only when `skippedInProgress > 0 && drained === 0`, so normal drains that successfully recover entries are unaffected. The SDK contract at `dist/delivery-queue-runtime-Dv-0a9G_.js:13` preserves `drainPendingDeliveries` as `Promise<void>` for backward compatibility while exposing the additive result-bearing variant at line 22. The delayed regression test at 46s proves the cooldown survives past the HTTP request timeout, and the test at 61s proves the cooldown correctly expires.
- **What was not tested:** Live Telegram send stall scenario requiring a real Telegram bot with network conditions that cause 129s+ stalls. The cooldown duration (60s) covers two long-poll intervals to handle delayed poll-success; production tuning may adjust it.

Closes #89953

target.title: fix(update): hand off supervised auto-updates
target.body:
## Linked context

Closes #78492.

## Summary

- Route supervised startup auto-updates through the existing managed-service update handoff instead of spawning `openclaw update` inside the live gateway service process.
- Move the handoff implementation to `src/infra/` so both startup auto-update and `update.run` can share the same lifecycle code, while keeping the gateway import path as a re-export.
- Pass the configured update channel, timeout, and `OPENCLAW_AUTO_UPDATE=1` through the handoff command, keep the existing CLI package-update guard intact, and avoid creating an undeliverable restart continuation for unattended auto-update.

## AI assistance disclosure

I used AI assistance to inspect the issue, compare the prior closed PR, implement the patch, and run validation. I reviewed and understand the affected startup updater, managed-service handoff, restart scheduling, and CLI package-update guard paths.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: supervised gateway startup auto-update no longer runs the package update as a direct child of the live gateway service process with inherited service markers.

Real environment tested: local Linux user-systemd service using a packaged OpenClaw tarball from this PR, a throwaway npm prefix, isolated state/config, a unique profile-specific unit, a throwaway loopback port, and a throwaway token. The service started from package version 2026.6.1 and auto-updated through the beta channel to package version 2026.6.2-beta.1.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
node .artifacts/issue-78492/run-systemd-package-proof.mjs
```

Evidence after fix:

Packaged service and update result:

```text
Unit: openclaw-gateway-proof784920adccb.service
Package before: 2026.6.1
Package after: 2026.6.2-beta.1

systemctl --user show:
MainPID=2744480
ExecMainCode=0
ExecMainStatus=0
Id=openclaw-gateway-proof784920adccb.service
ActiveState=active
SubState=running

managed handoff log:
[2026-06-04T18:06:36.961Z] starting managed update command: openclaw update --yes --channel beta --timeout 2700
[2026-06-04T18:06:36.963Z] managed update command pid=2744165
{
  "status": "ok",
  "mode": "npm",
  "root": "<proof-root>/npm-prefix/lib/node_modules/openclaw",
  "before": { "version": "2026.6.1" },
  "after": { "version": "2026.6.2-beta.1" },
  "steps": [
    {
      "name": "global update",
      "command": "npm i -g --prefix <proof-root>/npm-prefix/lib/node_modules/.openclaw-update-stage-... openclaw@beta --no-fund --no-audit --loglevel=error --min-release-age=0",
      "exitCode": 0
    },
    {
      "name": "global install swap",
      "command": "swap <proof-root>/npm-prefix/lib/node_modules/.openclaw-update-stage-.../lib/node_modules/openclaw -> <proof-root>/npm-prefix/lib/node_modules/openclaw",
      "exitCode": 0
    },
    {
      "name": "openclaw doctor",
      "command": "/usr/bin/node <proof-root>/npm-prefix/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/index.js doctor --non-interactive --fix",
      "exitCode": 0
    }
  ],
  "durationMs": 19607
}
[2026-06-04T18:07:09.355Z] managed update command exited code=0 signal=null
```

Gateway health after the package update and service restart:

```json
{
  "ok": true,
  "plugins": {
    "loaded": [
      "browser",
      "canvas",
      "device-pair",
      "file-transfer",
      "memory-core",
      "phone-control",
      "talk-voice"
    ],
    "errors": []
  },
  "sessions": {
    "path": "<proof-root>/state/agents/main/sessions/sessions.json",
    "count": 0,
    "recent": []
  }
}
```

Observed result after fix: a packaged user-systemd gateway started from this PR handed startup auto-update to the detached managed-service updater, ran the real `openclaw update --yes --channel beta --json` path through npm, swapped the package root, ran doctor successfully, restarted the gateway service, and returned healthy.

What was not tested: macOS launchd and Windows Scheduled Task proofs were not run. This proof covers the packaged Linux user-systemd path that ClawSweeper asked for; the existing focused tests still cover the helper-level channel, timeout, marker-stripping, and restart-continuation behavior.

Before evidence (optional but encouraged): ```
✓ without this patch, the gateway process printed an undeliverable 'gateway service restarted by auto-update' wall message and the supervisor saw a no-op restart that could stall further commands
```

## Tests and validation

```bash
pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test:fast  # all passing
```

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`) Yes — unattended auto-update no longer produces an undeliverable restart continuation message, and the gateway process no longer runs the update command as a direct child with inherited service markers.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`) No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`) No.

What is the highest-risk area? The managed-service handoff path runs inside the live gateway service. If the handoff command fails or blocks, the gateway could stall or enter an inconsistent startup state.

How is that risk mitigated? The handoff uses the existing managed-service `spawnManagedService` helper with a timeout, same as `update.run`. The timeout prevents indefinite blocking, and the CLI package-update guard still applies on the handoff target.

## Current review state

Next action: maintainer review.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof? Maintainer review.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed? ClawSweeper asked for packaged launchd or systemd proof; the updated proof above adds a real packaged Linux user-systemd auto-update run.

target.title: fix(discord): extend component callback ttl
target.body:
## Summary

- Raise the default Discord component callback registry TTL from 30 minutes to 7 days.
- Expand the bounded `agentComponents.ttlMs` override maximum from 24 hours to 30 days.
- Refresh Discord config docs, UI hints, generated config metadata, and baseline hashes.
- Add registry-level coverage proving the default remains usable until the 7-day boundary and expires afterward.

This makes durable Discord review and approval cards usable by default without changing component authorization, reusable-component semantics, or Discord interaction handling outside registry TTLs.

## Why seven days is a good default

Discord component messages are not ephemeral UI popups. They remain in the conversation and are commonly used as durable review cards: release approvals, incident follow-ups, access reviews, workflow decisions, and other async prompts where a user may reasonably respond after meetings, overnight, after a weekend, or across time zones.

The current 30-minute fallback makes those visible controls fail while the message still looks actionable. That creates a poor operator experience: the user clicks a still-visible approval card, the registry entry is gone, and the workflow has to be retried or reposted. Reposting replacement cards just to refresh callback state creates duplicate messages and makes the original controls look broken.

Seven days is intended to cover one normal weekly review cycle. It is long enough for Friday-to-Monday and cross-time-zone workflows, but still finite and much shorter than the lifetime of the Discord message itself. It also avoids requiring every Discord account to discover and set `agentComponents.ttlMs` before async review cards work reliably.

The 30-day value is only the schema maximum. It is an explicit operator override ceiling for deployments that choose a longer callback window; it is not the default. Deployments that want shorter callback lifetimes can set `agentComponents.ttlMs` lower.

## Safety model

This PR changes the freshness window for callback registry entries; it does not create a new authorization path.

- Component authorization and per-entry `allowedUsers` checks still run before callbacks dispatch.
- The PR does not add permissions, secrets, new network access, or new tool execution behavior.
- Persistent component and modal state remains count-bounded by `maxEntries: 500` per namespace.
- The plugin state store sweeps expired rows and evicts oldest live rows above `maxEntries` on register, so the longer TTL does not make durable storage unbounded.
- On high-volume servers, the 500-entry count cap can become the effective lifetime bound before the 7-day TTL, which fails closed by making older callbacks stop resolving sooner.
- The process-local in-memory registry is not the durable retention boundary, and this PR does not change its semantics.

The remaining tradeoff is explicit: old visible Discord messages can remain actionable for longer, but only through the existing authorization gates and within finite TTL/count bounds. That is the intended product tradeoff for making async review cards work without per-deployment config.

## Linked context

Closes #90390

Related #84184 and #84189, which added the configurable TTL field while preserving the old 30-minute default.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Discord component callbacks without an explicit `agentComponents.ttlMs` expired after 30 minutes by default, which is too short for asynchronous review and approval messages.
- Real environment tested: OpenClaw source checkout with a standalone registry invocation, Discord registry/config/send tests, generated config metadata checks, and changed-lane type/lint checks. No live Discord multi-day click was performed.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```sh
node --import tsx --input-type=module
```

The command imported `extensions/discord/src/components-registry.ts`, registered a component without `ttlMs`, moved the process clock to the default-TTL boundary, and resolved the entry before and after expiry.

- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):

Terminal output from the standalone registry invocation:

```json
{
  "source": "extensions/discord/src/components-registry.ts",
  "registeredWithoutTtlMs": true,
  "defaultTtlMs": 604800000,
  "resolvesAtSevenDaysMinusOneMs": true,
  "resolvesAtSevenDaysPlusOneMs": false,
  "observedExpiresAtDeltaMs": 604800000
}
```

- Observed result after fix: the real registry module keeps an entry registered without `ttlMs` resolvable until just before the 7-day boundary and stops resolving it just after that boundary. The focused Discord test run also passed 3 files and 67 tests.
- What was not tested: live Discord button clicks over a multi-day wall-clock interval.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: the live interval is intentionally not attempted in this small PR; the standalone registry invocation and registry clock-boundary test cover the expiry logic deterministically, and send-path tests continue to cover configured TTL propagation.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): current main defined `DEFAULT_COMPONENT_TTL_MS = 30 * 60 * 1000`, documented default `1800000`, and capped `agentComponents.ttlMs` at `86400000`.

## Tests and validation

Commands run after this patch:

- `pnpm test extensions/discord/src/components.test.ts extensions/discord/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/discord/src/send.components.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose`
- `pnpm config:channels:check`
- `pnpm config:docs:check`
- `pnpm check:changelog-attributions`
- `git diff --check`
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions`
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions:test`
- `pnpm lint:extensions`
- `pnpm tsgo:core`
- `pnpm tsgo:core:test`
- `pnpm lint:core`
- `pnpm check:no-conflict-markers`

Regression coverage added or updated:
